Summary: Reordered the four paragraphs so the contribution is stated first, related work follows to position it, then experimental protocol, then setup details and results — fixing the current ordering which buries the thesis in the final paragraph and splits experimental protocol from setup.

Diagnosis of the current ordering

The paragraphs as given run: (1) training setup, (2) related work, (3) protocol + results, (4) contribution. Three problems follow from this:

- The contribution — the value-of-information gating rule — appears last, after the reader has already been asked to interpret numerical results without knowing what the method is.
- Setup (paragraph 1) and protocol (paragraph 3) describe the same experiments but are separated by an unrelated related-work paragraph.
- Related work is placed between two empirical paragraphs, where it interrupts rather than frames.
- Results are reported (paragraph 3) before the method that produced them is named (paragraph 4), so the comparison to option-critic has no referent on first read.

Recommended order

1. Contribution (current paragraph 4) — lead with the value-of-information gating rule and the tau(s) threshold. State up front that it is ~50 lines on top of PPO and that it is what drives the low-budget gains.
2. Related work (current paragraph 2) — now positioned to contrast the new criterion against option-critic, feudal networks, meta-learned subroutines, and language-conditioned RL. The "delegates on a fixed schedule rather than reasoning over when to delegate" sentence becomes the direct hinge into the contribution just stated.
3. Experimental protocol (first half of current paragraph 3) — ProcGen 200-level training, full-distribution eval, and the k ∈ {1, 3, 10} delegation-budget constraint. Stop before the numeric results.
4. Training setup (current paragraph 1) — PPO hyperparameters, 25M steps, 5 seeds, hardware, encoders. This is reference material; placing it after protocol lets the reader skim it.
5. Results (second half of current paragraph 3) — DoorKey 0.71 vs 0.58 at k=3, ProcGen 0.42 vs 0.31, and the k=1 widening to 0.63 vs 0.41. The claim that "delegation timing matters more under tighter budgets" now lands as confirmation of the gating-rule thesis introduced in step 1.

Smaller adjustments this reordering enables

- The final sentence of the current contribution paragraph ("what enables the k=1 results above") references results that, after reordering, come after rather than before — change "above" to "reported below" or drop the back-reference entirely.
- The current paragraph 2's closing ("We discuss the relationship of our method to these lines of work in the next section") becomes redundant once related work directly follows the contribution; delete it.
- Consider merging setup and protocol into a single "Experimental setup" subsection with protocol first and hyperparameters/hardware second, so the empirical block reads as one unit rather than two.

No uncertainty to disclose — recommendations are structural and verifiable against the artifact as given.
